text: Dense adhesions from previous surgery are visible in the abdominal cavity. The surgeon begins adhesiolysis to prepare the area around the rectovaginal fistula for bowel resection.

The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis to separate the ileum from dense adhesions to the sigmoid colon, a common step in bowel resection following previous surgeries. The ileum is gradually freed from the adhesions to facilitate tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the ileum from the lateral pelvic wall and sigmoid colon. The ileum is then completely detached from the psoas muscle, freeing the pelvis for bowel mobilization. The bladder, densely adherent to the anterior sigmoid colon, is also separated. The ureter is identified and isolated at the sacral promontory, and adhesions of the parasigmoid and pararectum are separated from the lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis at the sacral promontory, entering the retro-rectal plane to mobilize the bowel while sparing nerves. The posterior aspect is relatively free of adhesions, allowing dissection to proceed to the coccyx bone. Posterolateral dissection is performed, with pararectal fat being dissected away to isolate the bowel. Bladder adhesions are separated from the vaginal apex, and the bladder is pushed down to identify the vault margin. Similar dissection is conducted on the opposite side from the sacral promontory.

The surgeon has mobilized the bowel away from the posterior and posterolateral aspects, revealing the location of the fistula. The vaginal apex and rectum are aligned in one plane, and dissection is performed parallel to the fat to address the five millimeter fistula identified by MRI fistulogram.

The surgeon dissects parallel to the fat, ensuring the fistula remains adherent at the anterior aspect of the rectum and vagina. Electrocoagulation is used to perforate and open the bowel, revealing the anterior margin for further dissection below the fistula level. The Danone-Willers fascia is separated, and the fistula's opening guides the dissection. The vagina is opened and mobilized, freeing the rectum from adhesions, marking the end of the fistula preparation.

The surgeon performs complete mobilization at the level of the fistula using electrocoagulation to cut through dense adhesions. The fistula tract is opened, and the final end margins are dissected away. The rectum is visibly separated from the vagina, with the fistula's opening clearly seen on the anterior aspect of the rectum.
